High-scoring verb for education/gender topics: 'empower women'.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the process of making a law
Cambridge.example_sentence: The enactment of the new law took time.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Formal legal term for government topics; collocate 'enactment of law'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The machine is encasing the wires.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Use in process diagrams (Task 1) for manufacturing.
Descriptive adjective for Speaking Part 2 places; avoid overuse.

Cambridge.english_meaning: a small area surrounded by a different area
Cambridge.example_sentence: The village is an enclave within the city.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Geography-specific; use in Task 2 cultural diversity essays.
Better than 'got' in Speaking/Writing: 'encountered difficulties'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: Teachers encourage students to try hard.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Versatile verb; collocate 'encourage creativity/development'.
Abstract noun useful for parenting/education topics.
Adjective for Task 1 trends: 'encouraging results'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The tiger is an endangered species.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Must-know for environment Task 2; collocate 'endangered species'.
Formal noun (UK spelling); use in academic style, not 'try'.
Use sparingly; replace 'very long' in Speaking for impact.

Cambridge.english_meaning: to give the right to vote
Cambridge.example_sentence: The law will enfranchise more citizens.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Political vocabulary for government/democracy essays.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The game will engage the children.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Collocate 'engage with community/students' for Task 2.
Use for work/study contexts, not just marriage meaning.
Formal academic verb;代替 'cause' in Task 2 for higher band.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The training will enhance your skills.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Top-10 IELTS verb; collocate 'enhance performance/experience'.
Past participle perfect for Task 1 comparisons.
Descriptive for people/art; shows wide vocabulary.
Basic word – upgrade to 'derive enjoyment from' in Writing.
Use in Task 1 process/diagram descriptions.

Cambridge.example_sentence: He became enmeshed in the scandal.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Academic metaphor: 'enmeshed in bureaucracy'.
Replace 'very big' in all modules.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The enquiring mind is always learning.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: UK spelling 'enquiring mind' – good for personality descriptions.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The music will enrapture the audience.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Literary verb; use once in Speaking Part 2 for flair.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the process of improving or making richer
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Collocate 'cultural/personal enrichment' in education essays.

Cambridge.english_meaning: to join a course or organisation
Cambridge.example_sentence: She will enrol in the class.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: UK spelling; use for education topics.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The practice of enslaving people is illegal.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Strong historical/social term; use carefully in Task 2.

Cambridge.english_meaning: to involve in a complicated situation
Cambridge.example_sentence: Do not entangle yourself in the argument.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Metaphorical use impresses: 'entangled in debt'.
Perfect Speaking adjective for books/films.
Abstract noun for motivation topics; collocate 'show enthusiasm'.
Adverb adds fluency in Speaking Part 1 routines.
Intensifier for Task 2 concessions: 'not entirely convinced'.
Key for economy/business Task 2; plural noun.
Formal verb for Speaking about speech clarity.
Essential adjective; collocate 'environmental impact/protection'.

Cambridge.english_meaning: to imagine as a future possibility
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Formal Task 2 verb: 'I envisage a future where…'

Cambridge.english_meaning: to imagine as a future possibility
Cambridge.example_sentence: She can envision the finished product.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: US variant of envisage; don't mix both spellings.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The plan was envisioned years ago.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Past tense for future predictions in Writing.

Cambridge.example_sentence: She felt envy towards her friend.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Emotion noun for social psychology topics.

Cambridge.english_meaning: a substance that speeds up chemical reactions
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Science-specific; define briefly if used in Task 2.

Cambridge.english_meaning: a short section at the end of a book
Cambridge.example_sentence: The epilogue explained what happened later.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Literary term; useful in arts/culture essays.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The epiphytic plant grows on trees.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Technical biology term; shows topic-specific vocab in Reading.
Cohesive device for comparisons in Task 1 & 2.

Cambridge.example_sentence: We live in the digital era.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Use 'digital era' instead of 'time now'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The eradication of the disease is possible.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Formal noun for disease/poverty essays: 'eradication of malaria'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The sea can erode the coastline.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Metaphorical use scores high: 'erode trust'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The eroded rock formed strange shapes.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Past participle for describing changes over time.

Cambridge.example_sentence: Soil erosion is a major problem.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Both literal (soil) and figurative (values).

Cambridge.english_meaning: a sudden release of lava from a volcano
Cambridge.ielts_tip: For volcanoes and metaphors: 'eruption of anger'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The escalation of violence is worrying.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Formal noun for conflict/costs topics.
Use phrasal 'escape from routine' in Speaking.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the tendency to escape from reality
Cambridge.example_sentence: Reading is a form of escapism.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Abstract noun for media/entertainment Task 2.
Very formal; one use per essay impresses examiners.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The essence of the argument is clear.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Phrase 'in essence' useful for summarizing.
High-frequency Task 2 adjective; collocate 'essential for'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The two plans are essentially the same.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Cohesive adverb for paraphrasing arguments.

Cambridge.example_sentence: We estimate the cost at 5000 rupees.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Verb for Task 1 data when exact numbers absent.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The estimated time of arrival is 3 p.m.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Common in Task 1: 'estimated at 50%'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: In my estimation, the job will take two days.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Noun form less common; prefer 'estimate'.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the state of being no longer close
Cambridge.example_sentence: There was estrangement between the brothers.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Formal social topic vocab for family breakdown.
Adverb for ethics essays: 'ethically sourced'.
Collocate 'ethnic minority/diversity'.

Cambridge.english_meaning: a person who studies human cultures
Cambridge.example_sentence: The ethnographer lived with the tribe.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Topic-specific noun; define in essay if used.

Cambridge.example_sentence: Good etiquette is important at dinner.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Culture topic: 'business etiquette'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The criminal tried to evade the police.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Use 'evade taxes/responsibility' for formal tone.

Cambridge.example_sentence: We need to evaluate the results.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Academic verb for Task 2; implies critical thinking.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The committee is evaluating the proposals.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Present participle good for process descriptions.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The evaluation will take place next week.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Noun form common in education/work topics.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the process of turning liquid into vapour
Cambridge.example_sentence: Evaporation occurs when water is heated.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Science process term for Task 1 diagrams.
Hyphenated adjective for justice/governance essays.

Cambridge.example_sentence: We will eventually reach the goal.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Cohesive sequencer better than 'at last' in Writing.
Both literal trees and metaphor 'evergreen topic'.

Cambridge.english_meaning: facts or information that prove something
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Uncountable in academic English; 'evidence shows' not 'evidences'.
Use structure 'It is evident that…' for Task 2.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the act of bringing a memory to mind
Cambridge.example_sentence: The music was an evocation of childhood.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Literary noun; shows sophisticated range.
Adjective for biology/change: 'evolutionary process'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The species will evolve over time.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Better than 'change slowly' in Task 2.
Past participle for technology descriptions.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The museum will exhibit the artefacts.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Verb for behaviour and museums; dual usage valuable.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The delay will exacerbate the situation.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Formal cause-effect verb for problems essays.
Adjective for work standards: 'exacting requirements'.

Cambridge.english_meaning: a very large unit of electrical capacitance
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Highly technical; avoid in IELTS unless science specialist.

Cambridge.english_meaning: to make something seem larger or more important
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Useful for media/advertising topics.

Cambridge.english_meaning: a statement that makes something seem larger
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Noun form for criticizing claims.
Archaeology/history vocabulary.
Noun collocates 'archaeological excavation'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The cost will exceed the budget.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Task 1 essential: 'exceeds 50%' not 'more than'.
Third-person singular for present data.
Abstract noun for education: 'pursuit of excellence'.

Cambridge.english_meaning: a case that does not follow the rule
Cambridge.example_sentence: There is no exception to the rule.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Phrase 'with the exception of' is cohesive.
Positive adjective stronger than 'very good'.

Cambridge.english_meaning: cases that do not follow the rule
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Plural form for generalizations.
Noun; don't confuse with adjective 'excessive'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The club is exclusively for members.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Adverb for narrowing scope in Task 2.

Cambridge.example_sentence: We went on an excursion to the mountains.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Formal alternative to 'trip' for travel Speaking Part 2.
Band 7+ formal verb for Task 2: 'execute a plan/policy/strategy'. Avoid in casual speaking; use 'carry out' instead.

Cambridge.english_meaning: relating to the management of a company
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Common in business Reading. Collocation: 'executive board/decision'. Don't confuse adjective with noun 'an executive'.
Perfect linking phrase for Writing Task 2: 'This is exemplified by...' - upgrades 'for example'.

Cambridge.english_meaning: to make a physical or mental effort
Cambridge.example_sentence: You need to exert more effort.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Academic collocation: 'exert pressure/influence'. Useful for environment and politics essays.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The long run will exhaust you.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Two meanings: tire and deplete. Task 2: 'exhaust natural resources' scores lexical resource.
Formal noun for Speaking Part 3 work/health. Phrase: 'suffer from exhaustion'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The gallery will exhibit the paintings.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: More formal than 'show'. Task 1: 'The chart exhibits a rising trend'.
Past form useful in process diagrams and Task 1 reports.

Cambridge.english_meaning: making one feel very happy and excited
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Band 8 adjective for Speaking Part 2 experiences. Collocation: 'exhilarating experience'.
Academic alternative to 'there is/are'. Use: 'Significant problems exist in...'

Cambridge.example_sentence: The existence of life on other planets is debated.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Frequent in academic topics: 'threaten the very existence of'. Use in Task 2 for environment/species.
Great for housing/cost essays. 'exorbitantly high prices' demonstrates collocation control.
Speaking travel topic. Pair with nouns: exotic food/location/species. Avoid for people.
Core Task 1 verb. Use transitive and intransitive: 'expand the business'.
Present continuous for trend descriptions: 'The market is expanding'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The city is expanding its boundaries.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Present continuous for trend descriptions: 'The market is expanding'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The expansion of the company is planned.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Noun form essential for Task 1/2: 'urban expansion', 'rapid expansion'.
Two meanings. For IELTS prefer spatial: 'expansive plains'. Less common for talkative.
Key phrase: 'life expectancy'. Uncountable in this collocation – IELTS favourite.

Cambridge.english_meaning: a belief that something will happen
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Plural common: 'meet/exceed expectations'. Useful in work/education topics.
Passive adjective: 'is expected to'. Perfect for Task 1 predictions.
Academic plural noun. Use for history/science Reading: 'scientific expeditions'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The school may expel the student.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Formal: 'expel from school/country'. Collocates with students and pollutants.
Formal noun for Task 1 pie charts: 'government expenditure on...'.
Countable: 'conduct/do an experiment'. Common in science passages.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The chef likes to experiment with flavours.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Verb + with: 'experiment with new methods'. Good for innovation topics.
Adjective for research: 'experimental group/design'. Task 2 technology essays.
Uncountable noun. 'Animal experimentation' – common ethical debate topic.
Uncountable. Collocation: 'technical expertise', 'area of expertise'. Speaking Part 3 jobs.

Cambridge.english_meaning: a statement that makes something clear

Cambridge.ielts_tip: Offer/give an explanation. Use to paraphrase 'why' in Task 2.
Academic contrast pair: explicit vs implicit – great for Reading and Task 2.
Past tense useful metaphorically: 'population exploded'. Task 1 rapid increase.
Two connotations: neutral 'exploit resources' and negative 'exploit workers'. Choose carefully.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The company is exploiting cheap labour.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Present participle for ongoing actions in Task 2 environment essays.

Cambridge.english_meaning: to travel to find out about a place

Cambridge.ielts_tip: Academic verb: 'explore issues/possibilities'. Perfect for essay introductions.
Literal and figurative: 'explosive growth' – strong collocation for Task 1.

Useful for time and space: 'extend deadline/range'.

High-frequency academic adjective: 'extensive research/damage'.
Adverb upgrades 'a lot': 'studied extensively'.

Transitive verb. Agriculture topic: 'fatten livestock'.

Uncountable noun. 'give/receive feedback' – essential education/work topic.

Metaphorical use: 'problems fester' – advanced Task 2 social issues.

Cambridge.ielts_tip: Art/photo description. Task 1 maps/process: 'in the foreground'.
Collocations: 'foreign language/investment'. Avoid negative 'alien' sense.
Formal adjective: 'foremost expert', 'first and foremost' – linking phrase.

Literary/academic verb. 'foreshadow future trends' – advanced Task 2.
Key IELTS distinction: formal vs informal language/register – Writing criteria.
Contrast pair former/latter – useful for referencing in Task 2.
Plural of formula. Maths/science. Note irregular: formulae/formulas both accepted.
Historical noun. Tourism descriptions: 'ancient fort'.
Adjective for events: 'forthcoming exam'. Also means communicative – avoid confusion.

Cambridge.english_meaning: a large strong building for defence
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Larger than fort. Collocation: 'impregnable fortress'. History/tourism topics.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the remains of ancient plants or animals

Band 7+ verb for Task 2: 'foster innovation/creativity/cooperation' scores higher than 'help'.

Past tense useful for describing policies: 'The programme fostered entrepreneurship.' Shows tense range.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the base on which something is built

Cambridge.example_sentence: Only a fraction of the students passed.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the process of breaking into small parts
Cambridge.example_sentence: Fragmentation of the land is a problem.

Adjective form: 'fragmented healthcare system' – criticize systems in Task 2.
Plural noun. Use 'fragments of evidence/information' – academic.
Descriptive for Speaking Part 2 (gardens). Avoid overuse in formal writing – use 'aromatic' instead.
Band 8 noun: 'legal/policy/theoretical framework' – essential for Task 2.
Rare word. Know phrase 'fraternal twins' for biology or 'fraternal bonds' for sociology.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the right to act or speak freely
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Abstract noun with 'of': freedom of speech/expression – classic Task 2 topic.
Logistics vocab: 'rail/air freight'. Useful for Task 1 transport diagrams.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The French fleets sailed to the new world.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Proper noun phrase – capitalize both words. Use only in historical essays.

Cambridge.english_meaning: fast and energetic in a wild way
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Strong adjective: 'frenetic pace of modern life' – perfect Speaking Part 3.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The child was frightened by the noise.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Adjective with 'by/of': frightened by noise. Use in narrative speaking.
Metaphorical: 'pushing the frontiers of science' – high-level Task 2.
Idiom: 'come to fruition' – use in conclusions for plans/projects.

Cambridge.english_meaning: feeling annoyed because of inability to change something
Cambridge.example_sentence: He felt frustrated with the delay.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Use 'frustrated by/with' – describes person. Contrast with 'frustrating' (situation).
-ing adjective for things: 'frustrating delay' – shows grammar control.
Uncountable: 'growing frustration among youth' – social issues vocabulary.

Cambridge.english_meaning: a person who is running away
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Formal legal term for crime topics. Pronunciation: /ˈfjuːdʒətɪv/.
Speaking Part 2 jobs: 'a fulfilling career' – Band 7+ collocation.

Cambridge.english_meaning: smoke or gas that smells unpleasant
Cambridge.example_sentence: The fumes from the factory are harmful.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Always plural. 'Toxic fumes' – environment and health.
Contrast with 'aesthetic' in design essays: functional vs beautiful.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The fundamental rules must be followed.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Academic booster: 'fundamental principle/difference/right'.

Cambridge.english_meaning: a ceremony for burying or cremating the dead
Cambridge.example_sentence: The funeral was attended by many.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Cultural topic – keep tone neutral and respectful.
Plural of fungus. Scientific – useful in Task 1 biology processes.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The fungus grows on the tree.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Singular form. Use article: 'a fungus'.
Informal. In writing prefer 'unclear' or 'ambiguous'.

Cambridge.english_meaning: a piece of cloth put over the mouth
Cambridge.example_sentence: The gag prevented him from speaking.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Also metaphorical: 'gag order' – legal topic.
Collocations: gain experience/knowledge/advantage – avoid 'gain money' (use earn).
Past for trends: 'gained popularity' – Task 1.
Present continuous: 'is gaining momentum' – shows dynamic language.
Specific weather term – demonstrates precise vocabulary.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The gallant soldier saved many lives.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Literary. Use in history/personality descriptions, sparingly.

Cambridge.english_meaning: to risk money on a game of chance
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Metaphor: 'gamble with the environment' – strong Task 2 argument.
Human plural – good for community/local economy topics.
High-level noun for food culture essays – Band 8.
'Data gathering' – academic phrase for research methods.
Technical plural – engineering Task 1.
Verb + at/into: 'gaze at the stars' – descriptive speaking.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The car is in first gear.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Uncountable in this sense: 'sports gear', 'protective gear'.
Academic: 'generalized model'. Warning – avoid overgeneralization in Task 2.
Key verb: generate electricity/income/ideas/jobs.
Always plural in general use: 'genes carry information'.
'Genetic modification/engineering' – frequent IELTS science topic.
Singular scientific: 'the human genome'.
Plural for comparative studies.

Cambridge.english_meaning: in a soft and careful way
Cambridge.example_sentence: She spoke gently to the child.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Manner adverb useful in Speaking Part 2 storytelling.
Academic: 'geographical distribution/location'.

Cambridge.english_meaning: relating to the study of the earth
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Collocation: 'geological formation/period'.
Uncountable discipline name – capitalize only at sentence start.
Modern preference is 'geometric' – both accepted in IELTS.
Biology verb; metaphor: 'ideas germinate'.

Cambridge.english_meaning: to use gestures to express ideas
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Formal verb – shows lexical range vs 'wave hands'.

Cambridge.english_meaning: movements of the hands or body
Cambridge.ielts_tip: 'Hand gestures' – cultural communication topic.
Both noun/adjective: 'tech giant', 'giant corporations'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The new gizmo is very useful.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Informal – avoid in Writing Task 2, OK in Speaking.

Cambridge.english_meaning: relating to ice or very cold
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Idiom: 'glacial pace' = extremely slow – great for Task 2.
Verb + at: 'glance at the chart' – Task 1 overview.
Strong negative verb for narratives.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The glazing of the windows is complete.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Technical: 'double glazing' – housing/energy topics.
Descriptive adjective for places in Speaking.

Cambridge.example_sentence: I caught a glimpse of the actor.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Phrase: 'catch a glimpse of' – natural collocation.

Cambridge.example_sentence: There was a glitch in the system.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Tech vocab: 'software glitch' – modern topic.
Emotive – better for Speaking than formal writing.
Cultural/religion – capitalize names: Goddess Lakshmi.
Specific example for tourism Speaking Part 2.
Economics: 'goods and services' – always paired.
Uncountable: 'gesture of goodwill' – diplomacy topic.
Essential Task 1 trend adverb: 'increased gradually'.
Gerund: 'after graduating from university' – common phrase.
'Grasp a concept' – academic collocation better than 'understand'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The cows graze in the field.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Animal context; also 'graze your knee' = scrape – note dual meaning.
Band 9 adjective: 'groundbreaking research/discovery'.
Hyphenated compound – science writing, use with noun.
Strong – use cautiously, avoid graphic detail in IELTS.

Cambridge.english_meaning: to complain in a bad-tempered way
Cambridge.example_sentence: He began to grumble about the weather.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Informal verb – Speaking, not Writing.
Sound verb – good for describing animals/people in stories.

Cambridge.english_meaning: to promise that something will happen
Cambridge.example_sentence: The company will guarantee the product.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Verb + noun: guarantee success/safety/quality – certainty language.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The old coin is a guilder.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Historical term – define for reader: 'former Dutch guilder'.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the natural home of an animal or plant
Cambridge.example_sentence: The forest is the habitat of many animals.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: 'Natural habitat' – core environment collocation.
Often negative: 'habitual smoker/offender'.
Formal past: 'production was halted' – passive voice practice.

Cambridge.example_sentence: Only a handful of people came.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: 'A handful of people' – better quantifier than 'some'.
Academic criticism: 'haphazard planning/development'.
US spelling 'harbors' – IELTS accepts both US/UK but be consistent.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The ship is in the harbour.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: UK spelling 'harbour' – prefer British English in IELTS.
Usually plural: 'economic hardships'.
Psychology: 'hardwired response/behavior'.
'Harness renewable energy' – perfect environment collocation.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The process of harnessing wind power is growing.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Gerund form useful for process descriptions.
Collocates with 'criticize, punish, judge harshly'.
Noun/verb both common: 'harvest crops' – agriculture topic.

Cambridge.english_meaning: to come out of an egg
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Biology; idiom 'hatch a plan' is informal – avoid in writing.
British business term – 'haulage company'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The road is a hazard in the rain.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: 'Health/safety hazard' – common compound noun.
Comparative – use in lifestyle/health essays with 'than'.
Homonym: 'hearing' (ability) vs 'court hearing' – check context.
'Heartily laugh/agree' – slightly formal speaking phrase.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the language of the Jewish people
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Proper noun – always capitalize.
Use in Task 1 maps: 'covers 5 hectares'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: We live in the northern hemisphere.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Geography: 'Northern/Southern Hemisphere' – capitalize.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The news will herald a new era.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Formal verb: 'herald a new era' – strong introductions.

Cambridge.english_meaning: relating to plants used for medicine
Cambridge.ielts_tip: 'Herbal medicine/remedies' – health alternative topic.
Plural noun – agriculture controversy topic.
Scientific classification – use with animals.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the passing of characteristics from parents
Cambridge.example_sentence: Heredity plays a role in eye colour.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Uncountable: 'heredity plays a role' – nature vs nurture.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The heritability of the trait is high.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Technical statistics term – use only if describing research accurately.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The city has a rich cultural heritage.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: IELTS Culture topic staple. Use 'cultural heritage' or 'UNESCO World Heritage site' in Task 2 essays about tourism vs preservation for Band 8 vocabulary.
Best used metaphorically in Speaking Part 2: 'a minor hiccup in the project'. Too informal for academic Writing.
Plural form fits Task 2 about workplace/society: 'rigid social hierarchies'. Shows academic precision.

Cambridge.example_sentence: There is a hierarchy in the organisation.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Key term for organization essays. Collocation: 'corporate hierarchy, social hierarchy' – use instead of 'levels'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The report will highlight the problem.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Perfect Task 1 verb. 'The graph highlights a dramatic increase' scores higher than basic 'shows'.
Very formal C2 adverb. Use once in Writing Task 2 for effect: 'hitherto overlooked problem'. Avoid in Speaking.
Past verb useful for process/narrative: 'the flag was hoisted'. Passive voice demonstrates grammar range.
Environment Task 2 gold. 'Decline in honeybees threatens biodiversity' shows specific topic vocabulary.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the line where the sky meets the earth
Cambridge.example_sentence: The sun set on the horizon.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Idiomatic use boosts Speaking: 'broaden my horizons'. Literal use fits travel descriptions.
Task 1 diagrams/processes: 'placed horizontally'. Shows precise spatial language.
Adverb for describing layouts. 'The bars extend horizontally' – good for Map tasks.
Stronger than 'very bad'. Use in Speaking Part 3 about accidents: 'a horrific crash' – lexical resource Band 8.

Cambridge.english_meaning: friendly and generous treatment of guests
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Tourism essay core word. Collocation: 'hospitality industry, renowned for hospitality'.
Academic adjective: 'hostile environment/workplace'. Useful for Task 2 social issues.

Cambridge.english_meaning: a heated building for growing plants
Cambridge.example_sentence: The plants grow in the hothouse.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Technical noun for agriculture tasks. Metaphor: 'a hothouse for innovation' – advanced.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The hull is made of steel.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Specialist ship term. Use only in transport/process descriptions, not general essays.
Band 9 global issues vocab. 'humanitarian aid/crisis' – perfect for Task 2.
Education topic essential. Contrast: 'funding humanities vs STEM'.
Use for strong conclusions: 'benefits humanity as a whole' – more formal than 'people'.
Animal rights essays: 'trophy hunting, hunting ban' – key collocations.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The first hurdle is the exam.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Great metaphor: 'overcome the biggest hurdle' – natural in Speaking Part 2 and Task 2.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the outer covering of a seed
Cambridge.example_sentence: Remove the husk from the rice.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Process Task 1 agriculture: 'remove the husk'. Low-frequency, topic-specific.

Cambridge.english_meaning: devices that use fluid to lift heavy objects
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Technical phrase shows precision in manufacturing process diagrams – Band 8 Task 1.
Culture/religion Speaking topic. 'traditional hymn' adds lexical depth.
Academic core. Collocate: 'formulate/test a hypothesis' – common in science Task 2.

Cambridge.english_meaning: a medicine for pain and inflammation
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Specific health vocab for Speaking Part 3. Shows precise lexical choice vs generic 'medicine'.
Politics/philosophy essays: 'youthful idealism' – high-level abstract noun.
Task 1 comparisons: 'remained identical'. More academic than 'the same'.
Universal academic verb. Task 1: 'identify trends', Task 2: 'identify causes'.
Education essays: 'ignorance about climate change'. Formal abstract noun.

Cambridge.example_sentence: He is ignorant of the facts.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Can sound rude. In Writing, soften to 'largely ignorant of' or use 'uninformed'.
Health topic plural. 'chronic illnesses' – better than 'sicknesses' for Task 2.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the ability to form mental images
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Education/arts essays: 'stimulate children's imagination'. High-frequency abstract noun.
Adjective for describing solutions/art. 'imaginative approach' – Band 7+ descriptor.
Use in Speaking Part 2 hooks: 'Imagine living abroad...' – engaging opener.

Cambridge.example_sentence: There is an imbalance in the diet.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Task 2 key noun. 'gender imbalance, trade imbalance' – academic collocations.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The idea is imbedded in the mind.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Variant spelling of embedded. 'deeply imbedded in culture' – shows C1 vocabulary.

Cambridge.english_meaning: a copy or act of copying
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Art/business essays: 'cheap imitation'. Also 'imitation is the sincerest form of flattery' – idiom for Speaking.
Stronger than 'very big'. 'immense pressure' – boosts lexical resource in Task 2.
Adverb for emphasis: 'immensely popular' – better than 'very'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The child is immune to measles.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Health topic: 'immune system'. Figurative: 'immune to criticism' – advanced usage.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The impact of the news was huge.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Most overused IELTS word – use precisely. Collocate: 'profound/negative impact on'. Noun and verb.

Cambridge.example_sentence: It is imperative to act now.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Formal adjective for conclusions: 'It is imperative that governments act' – Band 9 structure.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The imperiality of the palace is striking.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Rare abstract noun. Prefer 'imperialism' in history essays. Use cautiously.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the process of putting a plan into action
Cambridge.example_sentence: The implementation of the policy took time.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Business/policy Task 2 core: 'successful implementation of the policy'.
Past participle for Task 1 processes or Task 2 solutions: 'measures were implemented'.
Academic noun: 'far-reaching implications'. Shows ability to discuss consequences.
C1 adjective. Contrast pair 'implicit vs explicit' impresses examiners in Task 2.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The statement implies that change is needed.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Academic reporting verb: 'this implies that...' – good for Task 1 conclusions.
Key for interpreting data: 'The data imply a correlation'.

Cambridge.english_meaning: an artist who paints in the impressionist style
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Arts topic specific: 'Impressionist movement'. Shows cultural knowledge.
Describing achievements: 'impressive growth' – better than 'very good'.
Speaking Part 2 storytelling: 'had to improvise' – natural phrasal use.

Cambridge.english_meaning: a sudden desire to do something
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Psychology/consumer essays: 'buy on impulse, impulsive buying'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The decision is in accordance with the law.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Formal phrase for Task 1/2. 'in accordance with the law' – boosts formality.
Process description: 'the system has been in operation since 2010'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: His inability to swim is not a problem.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Abstract noun for Task 2: 'inability to adapt' – more formal than 'can't'.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the state of being not enough
Cambridge.example_sentence: The inadequacy of resources is clear.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Academic: 'inadequacy of infrastructure' – Band 8 noun phrase.
Common in problem essays: 'inadequate funding/healthcare'.
Modern business verb. 'incentivizes recycling' – shows contemporary vocab.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The incessant rain lasted for days.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Descriptive adjective for noise/problems: 'incessant traffic' – C1 level.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The incidence of the disease is low.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Health/statistics: 'incidence of disease' – academic precision for Task 1.
Plural for safety reports: 'number of incidents fell'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: She has an inclination towards music.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Formal noun: 'natural inclination towards arts' – good for personality essays.
Use structure 'be inclined to': 'people are inclined to believe' – academic hedging.

Cambridge.english_meaning: to contain as part of a whole
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Basic but essential. Vary with 'encompass' in Task 1 for higher score.

Cambridge.example_sentence: Inclusion of all students is important.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Social policy term: 'social inclusion, financial inclusion' – Task 2.
Strong negative – use carefully in Writing. Better: 'largely incompetent management'.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the state of being out of place
Cambridge.ielts_tip: High-level noun: 'cultural incongruity' – shows lexical sophistication.

Cambridge.english_meaning: to include as part of a whole
Cambridge.example_sentence: The design will incorporate new features.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Academic verb: 'incorporate feedback/technology' – better than 'include'.
Overused in Speaking. In Writing, prefer 'remarkable' for formality.

Cambridge.english_meaning: to become subject to something bad
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Formal verb: 'incur costs/debt' – essential for economics Task 2.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The sign will indicate the way.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Core Task 1 verb for data. 'Figures indicate a decline'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: There is no indication of rain.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Noun form: 'clear indication of growth'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The results are indicative of success.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Academic adjective: 'indicative of a wider trend' – Band 9 phrase.
Social issues: 'public indifference to climate change'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: She seemed indifferent to the news.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Adjective: 'indifferent attitude' – precise lexical choice.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The indigenous people have lived here for centuries.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Culture/environment essential: 'indigenous people/languages/species' – high-scoring topic vocab.
Health Speaking: 'suffer from indigestion'. Specific vs generic 'stomach ache'.
Academic: 'virtually indistinguishable from' – excellent comparison phrase.
Key Task 2 noun/adjective. Contrast 'individual vs collective responsibility'.
Collocations: 'industrial revolution, industrial waste' – common IELTS themes.
Policy essays: 'proved largely ineffective' – formal evaluation.
Powerful adjective for conclusions: 'climate change is inevitable without action'.
More formal than 'cheap'. 'inexpensive alternative' – good Task 2.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The project is still in its infancy.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Idiom: 'in its infancy' – describe new technology/projects in Task 2.
Formal/medical term. 'infant mortality rate' – precise for health essays.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the invasion of the body by germs
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Health collocations: 'risk of infection, prevent infection'.

Cambridge.english_meaning: able to spread from person to person
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Distinguish from 'infective'. 'highly infectious disease' – accurate academic use.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the presence of large numbers of pests
Cambridge.example_sentence: There is an infestation of insects.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Environment topic: 'rat infestation' – specific pest vocabulary.
Two meanings: medical ('inflammatory response') and social ('inflammatory remarks') – both C1.
Past verb: 'heavily influenced by social media' – passive common.
Plural noun: 'cultural influences' – Task 2 media/education.
Describe people/ideas: 'highly influential figure'.
Describe sources: 'informative lecture/documentary' – good for Speaking Part 3.

Cambridge.english_meaning: basic structures like roads and bridges
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Urban development essential: 'transport/digital infrastructure' – Band 8 topic vocab.
Formal verb: 'infuse confidence/energy into' – metaphorical high-level usage.
Praise inventions: 'ingenious solution/design' – stronger than 'smart'.
Abstract noun: 'human ingenuity' – perfect for technology Task 2 conclusions.
Literal and figurative: 'key ingredient for success' – idiomatic Band 8.

Cambridge.english_meaning: a person who lives in a place
Cambridge.example_sentence: The inhabitant of the island is friendly.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: More formal than 'person who lives'. 'local inhabitants' – geography essays.
Medical/formal: 'inhalation of pollutants' – health environment Task 2.
Verb pair with exhale – useful process descriptions.
Finance/culture: 'cultural inheritance' – link to heritage.
Biology/social: 'inherited traits/wealth'.
Academic verb: 'inhibit growth/development' – science Task 2.
Cohesive device for Task 1/2 sequencing. Better than 'first'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The project was initiated last year.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Formal past: 'project was initiated' – passive voice.

Cambridge.english_meaning: the ability to act without being told
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Two uses: 'show initiative' (personal quality) and 'government initiative' (plan).
Poetic adjective: 'innermost thoughts/feelings' – good for Speaking Part 2 descriptive.
Technology/business core word. Collocation: 'technological innovation, drive innovation'.

Cambridge.example_sentence: The name is inscribed on the stone.
Cambridge.ielts_tip: Culture/history: 'inscribed on the monument' – precise verb.
Noun form: 'ancient inscription' – archaeology/culture essays.
